characters.blurb: King of Sparta whose death left a pregnant queen. Lycurgus ruled as guardian until Charilaus was born.
Son of Polydectes. Lycurgus placed the child on the throne and later fled suspicion—then returned with the great reforms.
Named among Lycurgus’s ancestors in the Eurypontid line; part of the tangled royal pedigree Plutarch rehearses.
Early king who pressed Helots and Arcadians; cited to show how martial the house was before Lycurgus’s laws.
Link in the Eurypontid king-list Plutarch gives when tracing Lycurgus’s blood.
Elian who, with Lycurgus, is said to have ordered the Olympic games and the sacred armistice.
Later Spartan king who instituted (or strengthened) the ephorate—the check on kings that tempered Lycurgus’s design.

characters.relation: King who tried to restore the laws
characters.blurb: Reformer-king who sought to revive Lycurgus’s equality; Plutarch’s fuller tale of him is another Life.

characters.relation: Youth who struck out his eye
characters.blurb: Angry young Spartan who blinded Lycurgus in one eye during a riot over the messes; Lycurgus made him a household pupil instead of punishing him.
Named with Theopompus in traditions about amending the Great Rhetra after the people grew bold in assembly.
The Delphic “constitution” Lycurgus brought home: tribes, elders, assembly—and later a clause curbing popular amendments.
Through Delphi Lycurgus sought divine warrant for his laws; the rhetra is cast as Apollo’s voice.
One of the two places named in the rhetra between which the Spartan assembly was to meet.
Stream or bridge paired with Babyca as the bounds of the Lycurgan assembly ground.
Subject population bound to the land. Lycurgus’s system rested on their labor—and on the crypteia’s terror.
Lesbian lyre-poet said to have settled civic strife at Sparta with music—an art Lycurgus’s city still prized.
Elegist whose marching songs stiffened Spartan courage; cited among the cultural props of the Lycurgan city.
Musician-sage Plutarch links to the civilizing arts Sparta admitted even while distrusting fancy rhetoric.
Fifth-century general Plutarch cites as proof that Lycurgus’s breed of austere courage still appeared in later wars.
Often invoked when Plutarch weighs Sparta’s mixed constitution against ideal schemes.

characters.relation: Critic of parts of the system
characters.blurb: Noted flaws in Spartan women, property, and ephors even while granting Lycurgus’s stature as a lawgiver.
Both Spartan royal houses claimed Heraclid blood—the pedigree that framed Lycurgus’s right to reshape the state.
Author whose anecdotes about Lycurgus’s travels and death Plutarch sometimes follows.
Cited for the story that Lycurgus starved himself abroad so Sparta would not alter his laws.
Appears among later Spartans whose careers show the city’s long martial afterlife under Lycurgan habits.

Spartan reformer who—whether by oracle, travel, or force of character—gave his city its lasting constitution of equality, common meals, and trained courage. He starved himself abroad at the last so Sparta could never undo its oath to keep his laws.
The three hundred at Thermopylae are Plutarch's proof that Lycurgus's training produced men who could face death as a duty; Leonidas is the supreme example of the laws' endurance.

characters.relation: Great Spartan king; the laws' finest product
characters.blurb: Cited as the living proof that Lycurgus's discipline still produced great commanders centuries later—and that Sparta's decline set in when wealth broke the customs.

characters.relation: Ancestor; founder of the royal line
characters.blurb: Early king of Sparta from whom the Eurypontid house took its name—the dynastic line Plutarch traces when establishing Lycurgus's right to reform the state.

characters.relation: Navarch who bent the laws with wealth
characters.blurb: Brilliant admiral whose victory at Aegospotami brought silver and gold flooding into Sparta—the wealth Lycurgus's iron currency was meant to exclude, and the beginning of decline.
Plutarch says Lycurgus found Homer's works in Ionia—or had them from Creophylus's descendants in Samos—and was the first to bring them to Greece in ordered form.
Noted that Sparta, stripped of its monuments, would seem small to later ages—while its true strength, the disciplined city Lycurgus built, would astonish them.
Called Sparta a city of divine men who held elder-counsel, young-courage, and choruses of the Muses—a phrase Plutarch cites to show what the whole Greek world saw in the Lycurgan order.

characters.relation: Patron goddess of Sparta's Bronze House
characters.blurb: Athena Poliachos was worshipped at the famous Bronze House on the Spartan acropolis—the civic goddess whose temple framed Lycurgus's founding dedications.
Sicilian historian cited when Plutarch tries to fix the generation in which Lycurgus lived—a question without a secure answer even in antiquity.

characters.relation: Ancient Spartan cited on divorce law
characters.blurb: A very old Spartan Plutarch quotes on the customs concerning divorce and women—his answers, laconic to the point of comedy, illustrate the Lycurgan spirit perfectly.

characters.relation: Spartan elder; example of Laconic wit
characters.blurb: Cited for one of the memorable one-line Spartan answers Plutarch collects—the kind of compressed wisdom Lycurgus's culture prized above all rhetorical flourish.

characters.relation: Musical theorist who noted Sparta's songs
characters.blurb: Peripatetic writer on music cited by Plutarch as evidence that Sparta cultivated disciplined song and lyre—the arts Lycurgus admitted because they served courage, not pleasure.
Asked an old Spartan what above all preserved Sparta; the answer—contempt for wealth—sums up the Lycurgan principle Plutarch repeats throughout the Life.
Later counted among the Seven Wise Men of Greece; associated with the ephoralty—the office Theopompus added as a check on kings—and with the terse wisdom Lycurgus's city bred.

locations.relation: Home city; seat of the reformed constitution
locations.blurb: Born here to the Eurypontid royal house and here he imposed the Great Rhetra—common meals, iron money, and the agoge—returning from exile with laws that outlasted the lawgiver.

locations.relation: First stop in voluntary exile; source of law and music
locations.blurb: First arrived here in exile, studying Cretan government and persuading the lyric sage Thales to sail to Sparta, where his music proved more effective than laws at calming civil strife.

locations.relation: Ionian coast; discovery of Homer's poems
locations.blurb: Sailed from Crete to Asia to compare temperate Cretan customs with Ionian luxury; here first encountered Homer's poems in the hands of Creophylus's descendants and gathered them for Sparta.

locations.relation: Model for separating soldiers from tradesmen
locations.blurb: Egyptians reported that he voyaged here and borrowed their practice of keeping the soldier class apart from craftsmen and laborers—a separation he transplanted to Sparta.

locations.relation: Oracle that sanctioned the Great Rhetra
locations.blurb: Consulted Apollo here before the great reforms; the oracle called him beloved of the gods and more god than man, and returned with the Rhetra—Sparta's divine constitution.

locations.relation: Olympic games site; tradition of his death
locations.blurb: Associated with the Olympic truce Lycurgus helped Iphitus establish; Apollothemis said he came to Elis and died there, one of several traditions about his final journey.

locations.relation: One tradition for his place of death
locations.blurb: Some sources reported that Lycurgus died in Cirrha near Delphi—fitting end for the lawgiver who had received the constitution-making oracle from Apollo in that same sacred region.

locations.relation: Neighbor that failed to moderate royal power
locations.blurb: Named with Messene as neighbors whose insistence on strict royal prerogative brought calamity—the negative examples that justified Lycurgus's balanced constitution for Sparta.
Messenia's defeat provided Sparta with Helot labor, and the later Messenian revolt nearly destroyed the state—illustrating that Lycurgus's order rested on constant vigilance.

locations.relation: Foil to Spartan simplicity in later anecdotes
locations.blurb: Cited in anecdotes comparing Athenian litigiousness and luxury to Spartan order; a Lacedaemonian visiting Athens was said to marvel that a citizen had been fined for idleness.

locations.relation: Territory added by royal ancestor Sous
locations.blurb: Ancestor Sous pushed Spartan dominion into Arcadia through conquest; Lycurgus inherited a state already practiced in border warfare before reshaping it with laws of equality.
